preFix.title: Repairing crumbling chimney breast plaster
preFix.subtitle: Strip failed plaster from a chimney breast and re-render
preFix.excerpt: How to tackle a chimney breast where salts and damp have caused the plaster to crumble: hacking off the failed material, treating the salt-contaminated brickwork, applying a specialist render coat, and finishing with a skim.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Standard BS EN 998-1 (specification for mortar for masonry).

content.text: A capped chimney breast often develops crumbling plaster. The cause is

left in the brickwork from years of use. When moisture enters the closed flue, the salts migrate outward and push the plaster off the wall. Painting over the failure or patching with standard filler does not solve it; the salts keep working and the surface fails again within months. Hack off the failed plaster and re-plaster with a salt-tolerant render system.

If the chimney is capped but not ventilated, fit a purpose-made ventilated cap or a chimney cowl with an air grille before plastering. A closed chimney stack traps moisture. Open chimneys with working flues do not present this problem: the rising warm air dries the brickwork.
Step 1, hack off the failed plaster

Protect the floor and adjacent surfaces with dust sheets. Hack off the plaster with a bolster chisel and club hammer, working from the top of the chimney breast down. Remove all loose and hollow plaster. Tap the sound-seeming edges with a hammer handle: any hollow sound means that area is also failing and must come off. Remove plaster at least 300 mm beyond the visibly failed area to ensure the new render bonds to sound brickwork.

Brush all loose dust and debris from the exposed brick. Apply a salt inhibitor solution to the brickwork and the joints, following the manufacturer's instructions. Allow to dry completely, which takes at least 24 hours. Do not wet the wall with water to clean it: additional moisture increases the salt activity.
Prime the brickwork with the primer specified by the

manufacturer. Apply the renovation render in coats no thicker than 15 mm per coat. On a chimney breast, one or two coats are usually required depending on the depth of the original plaster. Score the surface of each coat with a devilling float to provide a key for the next coat. Allow each coat to cure for at least 24 hours before applying the next.

Once the renovation render has cured hard (typically 5 to 7 days), apply a standard multi-finish skim coat, 2 to 3 mm thick, and finish with a steel trowel. Allow the skim to dry and lighten in colour completely before painting, which usually takes 3 to 5 days in a normal room. Paint with a breathable mineral paint or a standard emulsion: avoid vinyl-heavy paints that trap moisture in the wall.

attrs.fix: Strip back the paint and apply a breathable mineral paint. Ensure the chimney is adequately ventilated.
attrs.cause: Insufficient drying time before painting, or a vapour-impermeable paint trapping moisture in the wall.
attrs.symptom: New plaster developing salt bloom within a year

attrs.fix: Keep the surface slightly damp by light misting with a water spray for the first 24 hours if conditions are hot and dry.
attrs.cause: Render coat applied too thick in one pass, or drying too fast in warm direct sunlight.
attrs.symptom: Renovation render cracking as it cures
